text.title: Spring Boot Layered Architecture Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules grounding the canonical layered Spring Boot 3 pattern. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Controllers MUST return Java record DTOs (`public record UserResponse(...)`); they MUST NOT return JPA entities. Mapping happens via MapStruct generated mappers inside the service layer.
rule.rationale: Returning entities leaks Hibernate proxy state, lazy associations, and internal schema; `@JsonIgnore` patches the symptom, not the cause.

rule.statement: Every service method that performs writes MUST be annotated `@Transactional`. Class-level `@Transactional(readOnly = true)` is the default; write methods override locally.
rule.rationale: Without `@Transactional` on multi-step writes, each JDBC call auto-commits; a partial failure leaves the database inconsistent with no rollback.

rule.statement: Every list endpoint MUST accept `Pageable` and return `Page<DTO>` (or `Slice`). `findAll()` without `Pageable` on growable tables is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Unbounded `findAll()` causes OOM and timeouts as tables grow; `Pageable` enforces a contract that callers paginate.

rule.statement: Passwords MUST be hashed via `PasswordEncoder` (BCrypt) inside the service layer. Controllers MUST NOT accept pre-hashed passwords from the client.
rule.rationale: Client-supplied hashes let an attacker replay an old hash; server-side hashing centralises the cost factor + algorithm choice.

rule.statement: `pom.xml` (or `build.gradle`) MUST configure the MapStruct annotation processor. Hand-coded DTO ↔ entity mappers are forbidden — they drift silently from the schema.
rule.rationale: Annotation-processor mappers fail at compile when a property goes missing; hand-coded mappers leak old field names into runtime.

rule.statement: Spring Boot 3 imports MUST be `jakarta.*` (`jakarta.persistence`, `jakarta.validation`). `javax.persistence` / `javax.validation` imports are forbidden in a Boot 3 codebase.
rule.rationale: Boot 3 migrated to Jakarta EE 9+ namespaces; mixing `javax.*` produces compile errors or — worse — silent runtime failures when both jars are on the classpath.
